name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
وَمَا كَانَ هَـٰذَا ٱلْقُرْءَانُ أَن يُفْتَرَىٰ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ وَلَـٰكِن تَصْدِيقَ ٱلَّذِى بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ وَتَفْصِيلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ لَا رَيْبَ فِيهِ مِن رَّبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٣٧)
```

```english
This Quran could not have been fabricated by anyone besides Allah; rather, it is a confirmation of what was [revealed] before it, and an elaboration of the Book, there is no doubt in it, from the Lord of all the worlds. (37)
```

```arabic
أَمْ يَقُولُونَ ٱفْتَرَىٰهُ ۖ قُلْ فَأْتُوا۟ بِسُورَةٍ مِّثْلِهِۦ وَٱدْعُوا۟ مَنِ ٱسْتَطَعْتُم مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (٣٨)
```

```english
Do they say, ‘He has fabricated it?’ Say, ‘Then bring a surah like it, and invoke whomever you can, besides Allah, should you be truthful.’ (38)
```

```arabic
بَلْ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِمَا لَمْ يُحِيطُوا۟ بِعِلْمِهِۦ وَلَمَّا يَأْتِهِمْ تَأْوِيلُهُۥ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ كَذَّبَ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ ۖ فَٱنظُرْ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٣٩)
```

```english
Indeed, they deny something whose knowledge they do not comprehend, and whose explanation has not yet come to them. Those who were before them denied likewise. So observe how was the fate of the wrongdoers! (39)
```

```arabic
وَمِنْهُم مَّن يُؤْمِنُ بِهِۦ وَمِنْهُم مَّن لَّا يُؤْمِنُ بِهِۦ ۚ وَرَبُّكَ أَعْلَمُ بِٱلْمُفْسِدِينَ (٤٠)
```

```english
Some of them believe in it, and some of them do not believe in it, and your Lord best knows the agents of corruption. (40)
```

```arabic
وَإِن كَذَّبُوكَ فَقُل لِّى عَمَلِى وَلَكُمْ عَمَلُكُمْ ۖ أَنتُم بَرِيٓـُٔونَ مِمَّآ أَعْمَلُ وَأَنَا۠ بَرِىٓءٌ مِّمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ (٤١)
```

```english
If they deny you, say, ‘My deeds belong to me and your deeds belong to you: you are absolved of what I do and I am absolved of what you do.’ (41)
```

```arabic
وَمِنْهُم مَّن يَسْتَمِعُونَ إِلَيْكَ ۚ أَفَأَنتَ تُسْمِعُ ٱلصُّمَّ وَلَوْ كَانُوا۟ لَا يَعْقِلُونَ (٤٢)
```

```english
There are some of them who prick up their ears at you. But can you make the deaf hear even if they do not exercise their reason? (42)
```

```arabic
وَمِنْهُم مَّن يَنظُرُ إِلَيْكَ ۚ أَفَأَنتَ تَهْدِى ٱلْعُمْىَ وَلَوْ كَانُوا۟ لَا يُبْصِرُونَ (٤٣)
```

```english
There are some of them who observe you. But can you guide the blind even if they do not perceive? (43)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَظْلِمُ ٱلنَّاسَ شَيْـًٔا وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱلنَّاسَ أَنفُسَهُمْ يَظْلِمُونَ (٤٤)
```

```english
Indeed Allah does not wrong people in the least; rather, it is people who wrong themselves. (44)
```

```arabic
وَيَوْمَ يَحْشُرُهُمْ كَأَن لَّمْ يَلْبَثُوٓا۟ إِلَّا سَاعَةً مِّنَ ٱلنَّهَارِ يَتَعَارَفُونَ بَيْنَهُمْ ۚ قَدْ خَسِرَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِلِقَآءِ ٱللَّهِ وَمَا كَانُوا۟ مُهْتَدِينَ (٤٥)
```

```english
On the day He will gather them [it will be] as if they had not remained [in the world] except for an hour of the day getting acquainted with one another. They are certainly losers who deny the encounter with Allah, and they are not guided. (45)
```

## (Commentary)

This is a return to the subject of the Quran: that it is a Book revealed from Allah, in which there is no doubt, and a formal instruction (تلقين) on the argument regarding this. These verses are connected to what preceded them in His saying: **قُلْ هَلْ مِنْ شُرَكائِكُمْ مَنْ يَهْدِي إِلَى الْحَقِّ قُلِ اللهُ يَهْدِي لِلْحَقِ** "Say, ‘Is there anyone among your partners who guides to the truth?’ Say, ‘Allah guides to the truth.’" (10:34). It has already been mentioned that part of His guidance toward the truth is His guiding people to His religion, which He approves of, through the path of revelation (الوحي) to His prophets and the Books He revealed to them—such as the Books of Nuh, Ibrahim, Musa, ‘Isa, and Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم. These verses mention this and establish the proof that the Quran is among those [Books] that guide to the truth. For this reason, they are alluded to alongside it where it is said: **وَلكِنْ تَصْدِيقَ الَّذِي بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ وَتَفْصِيلَ الْكِتابِ لا رَيْبَ فِيهِ مِنْ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ** "but it is a confirmation of what was [revealed] before it, and an elaboration of the Book, there is no doubt in it, from the Lord of all the worlds." (10:37).

At the end of these verses, there is a return to the mention of the Gathering (الحشر) — *the resurrection and assembly of all beings for judgment* — which is one of the primary objectives of the Surah, as previously noted.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَما كانَ هذَا الْقُرْآنُ أَنْ يُفْتَرى مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ** "This Quran could not have been fabricated by anyone besides Allah" (10:37) to the end of the verse. It has been previously noted that negating an attribute or a meaning by negating "being" (الكون) implies the negation of the very capacity (الشأن) and potential (الاستعداد) for it. This is more emphatic than merely negating the act itself. There is a difference between saying, "Zayd was not one to stand" (ما كان زيد ليقوم), and saying, "Zayd did not stand" (لم يقم) or "Zayd has not stood" (ما قام زيد). The first indicates that standing was not within Zayd’s capacity, nor was he prepared for it, while the second merely negates the act of standing from him. There are many instances of this in the Quran, such as His saying: **فَما كانُوا لِيُؤْمِنُوا بِما كَذَّبُوا بِهِ مِنْ قَبْلُ** "But they would not believe what they had denied before." (10:74), and His saying: **ما كُنْتَ تَدْرِي مَا الْكِتابُ وَلَا الْإِيمانُ** "You did not know what the Book is, nor what is faith" (42:52), and His saying: **وَما كانَ اللهُ لِيَظْلِمَهُمْ** "Allah would not wrong them" (29:40).

Thus, His saying: **وَما كانَ هذَا الْقُرْآنُ أَنْ يُفْتَرى مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ** is a negation of the very possibility of fabrication (الافتراء) regarding the Quran, as has been said, and it is more emphatic than negating its actual occurrence. The meaning is: It is not within the nature of this Quran, nor within its capacity, to be a fabrication from other than Allah that someone invents against Allah, the Glorified.

And His saying: **وَلكِنْ تَصْدِيقَ الَّذِي بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ** "but it is a confirmation of what was [revealed] before it" (10:37); that is, a confirmation of what is present and revealed of the Book, namely the Torah (التوراة) and the Gospel (الإنجيل), just as He narrated from the Messiah عليه السلام his saying: **يا بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ إِنِّي رَسُولُ اللهِ إِلَيْكُمْ مُصَدِّقاً لِما بَيْنَ يَدَيَّ مِنَ التَّوْراةِ** "O Children of Israel! Indeed I am the apostle of Allah to you, confirming what is before me of the Torah" (61:6). He described them as being "before it" (بين يديه) despite their temporal precedence because there were other Books besides these two, such as the Book of Nuh and the Book of Ibrahim عليه السلام. When the precedence of all of them is considered, those closest to it in time—the Torah and the Gospel—are described as being "before it."

It has been suggested that "what is before it" refers to future events such as the Resurrection (البعث), the Gathering (النشور), the Reckoning (الحساب), and the Recompense (الجزاء), but this is of no consequence.

And His saying: **وَتَفْصِيلَ الْكِتابِ** "and an elaboration of the Book" (10:37) is conjoined to "confirmation" (تصديق). What is meant by "the Book" (الكتاب), as indicated by the context, is the genus of the Heavenly Book revealed from Allah, the Glorified, to His prophets. Elaboration (التفصيل) — *the act of distinguishing and explaining the constituent parts of a whole* — is the creation of a distinction between its parts that were integrated into one another or folded within each other, through clarification and explanation.

In this, there is an indication that the Divine Religion (الدين الإلهي) revealed to His prophets عليه السلام is one; there is no difference within it except in terms of brevity (الإجمال) and elaboration (التفصيل). The Quran elaborates what others expressed briefly, as the Exalted said: **إِنَّ الدِّينَ عِنْدَ اللهِ الْإِسْلامُ** "Indeed, with Allah religion is Islam" (3:19).

Furthermore, the Noble Quran elaborates what was brief in the previous Heavenly Books and is a guardian (مهيمن) over them all, as the Exalted said: **وَأَنْزَلْنا إِلَيْكَ الْكِتابَ بِالْحَقِّ مُصَدِّقاً لِما بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ مِنَ الْكِتابِ وَمُهَيْمِناً عَلَيْهِ** "We have sent down to you the Book with the truth, confirming what was before it of the Book and as a guardian over it" (5:48). And His saying: **لا رَيْبَ فِيهِ مِنْ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ** "there is no doubt in it, from the Lord of all the worlds" (10:37); meaning, there is no doubt that it is from the Lord of all the worlds. The second clause serves as a justification (تعليل) for the first.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَمْ يَقُولُونَ افْتَراهُ قُلْ فَأْتُوا بِسُورَةٍ مِثْلِهِ** "Do they say, ‘He has fabricated it?’ Say, ‘Then bring a surah like it’" (10:38) to the end of the verse. The particle "or" (أَمْ) is disjunctive (منقطعة), meaning: "Nay, they say he fabricated it." The pronoun refers to the Quran. Describing a single Surah (سورة) as being "like the Quran" is evidence that the term "Quran" applies to both a large portion and a small portion of it.

The meaning is: Say to those who say he fabricated it: "If you are truthful in your claim, then bring a single Surah like this 'fabricated' Quran, and call upon whomever you can besides Allah for aid and support." For if it were a fabricated speech, it would be human speech, and it would be possible to produce its like. In this, there is an explicit challenge (تحد) with a single Surah from the Surahs of the Quran, whether long or short.

From this, several points become clear. First: the challenge is not restricted to a specific Surah, for they did not accuse only a part of the Quran of being a fabrication to the exclusion of others, but rather the whole of it. He charges them to bring a Surah like that which they claim he fabricated—and they claimed this regarding the entire Quran, not just a part.

One should not heed the opinion of those who say that the indefiniteness (التنكير) in "a Surah" (بِسُورَةٍ) is for magnification (التعظيم) or categorization (التنويع), meaning a Surah among those that mention the stories of the prophets and reports of the warnings of this world and the Hereafter—arguing that fabrication is only suspected in informative reports (الإخبار) rather than performative speech (الإنشاء). Nor should one heed those who say it refers to a long Surah like this one—Surah Yunus—in its inclusion of the foundations of religion, the promise, and the threat.

This is because the Quran, in all its verses, is attributed to Allah, the Glorified. There is no difference in this regard between what contains informative reports and what contains performative speech, nor between a long Surah and a short one, even down to a single verse. The accusation of fabrication can apply to the whole because it is a denial of the attribution pertaining to the whole.

Second: the verse does not challenge based on the eloquence (البلاغة) and fluency (الفصاحة) of the Quran alone. Rather, the context in this verse and other verses of challenge (التحدي) testifies that the challenge is based on the Quran's attribute of perfection (الكمال) and its quality of excellence (الفضيلة). This includes its containment of the core of Divine Gnosis (المعارف الإلهية), the comprehensive laws (جوامع الشرائع) consisting of devotional rulings (الأحكام العبادية), civil, political, economic, and judicial laws, noble ethics, refined manners, stories of the prophets and past nations, prophecies and reports of the Unseen (الأخبار الغيبية), descriptions of the angels, jinn, the heavens, and the earth, wisdom, exhortation, the promise and the threat, reports of the Beginning and the Return (البدء والعود), the strength of argument, the majesty of expression, and light and guidance—all without any part of it contradicting another. Add to this its reaching a level of eloquence and fluency that human hands fall short of attaining.

The researchers among the scholars of the early generations and those who followed them fell short when they restricted its inimitability (إعجاز) to its eloquence and fluency. They wrote books and authored treatises on this, which diverted them from contemplating its realities and deepening their understanding of its gnosis. It led them to consider the meanings as "matters cast upon the road," equal for the bedouin and the city-dweller, the commoner and the elite, the ignorant and the learned, and that the merit belongs to the composition of the word over the composition of the meaning, and that there is no value in what lies beyond that.

Yet Allah, the Exalted, has described it with every beautiful attribute relevant to the challenge, such as describing it as light (نور), mercy (رحمة), guidance (هدى), wisdom (حكمة), exhortation (موعظة), proof (برهان), a clarification for all things (تبيان لكل شيء), an elaboration of the Book (تفصيل الكتاب), a healing for the believers (شفاء للمؤمنين), a decisive word (قول فصل) that is not a jest, and that it is the "positions of the stars" (مواقع النجوم), and that there is no contradiction in it. He did not explicitly specify its eloquence alone.

He made the statement absolute: that they could not bring its like even if they called upon whomever they could besides Allah, and even if jinn and men gathered for that purpose and were supporters of one another—and He did not restrict the speech to eloquence and fluency.

We have detailed the discussion on the inimitability of the Quran in the commentary on His saying: **وَإِنْ كُنْتُمْ فِي رَيْبٍ مِمَّا نَزَّلْنا عَلى عَبْدِنا فَأْتُوا بِسُورَةٍ مِنْ مِثْلِهِ** "And if you are in doubt concerning what We have sent down to Our servant, then bring a surah like it" (2:23) in the first volume of this book.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **بَلْ كَذَّبُوا بِما لَمْ يُحِيطُوا بِعِلْمِهِ وَلَمَّا يَأْتِهِمْ تَأْوِيلُهُ** "No, they deny that whose knowledge they could not encompass, and whose interpretation has not yet come to them" (10:39) to the end of the verse. The verse explains the reality behind their lack of faith and their claim that it is a fabrication: they denied that part of the Quran whose knowledge they did not encompass, or they denied the Quran itself, whose knowledge they did not encompass. For it contains true gnosis of the nature of objective realities (العلوم الواقعية) that their knowledge cannot contain, and its interpretation (التأويل) has not yet come to them—that is, the interpretation of that which they denied—such that it would compel them to believe in it.

This is what the context of the meaning requires. Thus, His saying: **وَلَمَّا يَأْتِهِمْ تَأْوِيلُهُ** "and whose interpretation has not yet come to them" refers to the Day of Resurrection, as supported by His saying, the Exalted: **هَلْ يَنْظُرُونَ إِلَّا تَأْوِيلَهُ يَوْمَ يَأْتِي تَأْوِيلُهُ يَقُولُ الَّذِينَ نَسُوهُ مِنْ قَبْلُ قَدْ جاءَتْ رُسُلُ رَبِّنا بِالْحَقِّ فَهَلْ لَنا مِنْ شُفَعاءَ فَيَشْفَعُوا لَنا أَوْ نُرَدُّ فَنَعْمَلَ غَيْرَ الَّذِي كُنَّا نَعْمَلُ** "Do they await anything but its interpretation? The day its interpretation comes, those who had forgotten it before will say, ‘Our Lord’s apostles certainly brought the truth. Are there any intercessors to intercede for us? Or can we be sent back, so that we may act differently from what we did?’" (7:53).

This supports what we previously established in the commentary on His saying: **ابْتِغاءَ الْفِتْنَةِ وَابْتِغاءَ تَأْوِيلِهِ وَما يَعْلَمُ تَأْوِيلَهُ إِلَّا اللهُ** "seeking to cause unrest and seeking to interpret it. But no one knows its interpretation except Allah" (3:7) in the third volume of this book: that the meaning of **interpretation** (التأويل) — *the ultimate reality and objective source upon which a statement or concept rests* — in Quranic usage is the reality upon which a meaning depends, whether it be a ruling, a piece of gnosis, a story, or other objective realities, without it being of the nature of a [mental] meaning itself. Furthermore, the entire Quran and all it contains of gnosis, rulings, reports, or otherwise, has an interpretation (تأويل).

This is also supported by His subsequent saying: **كَذلِكَ كَذَّبَ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ** "In the same way did those who were before them deny" (10:39). The comparison implies that those polytheists before them also denied what their prophets called them to because they did not encompass its knowledge and its interpretation had not yet come to them. Thus, the components of the religious call brought by the other prophets—gnosis and rulings—have an interpretation, just as the gnosis and rulings of the Quran have an interpretation, without it being of the nature of concepts and the meanings of words as they imagined.

The sum of the meaning is that these polytheists, who accuse the Quran of being a fabrication, are like the polytheists and disbelievers of previous nations. They were confronted by matters of the religious call, with its gnosis and rulings, which they did not encompass with knowledge such that they could be certain and believe. Their ignorance drove them to deny them, while the day has not yet come when their interpretation and the reality of their matter will be manifested to them—a manifestation that would compel them toward certainty and belief. That is the Day of Resurrection, in which the veil will be lifted from the face of realities in their objective existence. These people denied and wronged, just as those before them denied and wronged; so look at what was the end of those wrongdoers, so that you may surmise what will befall these people.

This is what a precise investigation into the meaning of the verse yields. The commentators have various differing opinions based on their views regarding the meaning of "interpretation" (التأويل), but there is no benefit in addressing them here, as we have exhaustively examined their views previously.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ يُؤْمِنُ بِهِ وَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ لا يُؤْمِنُ بِهِ وَرَبُّكَ أَعْلَمُ بِالْمُفْسِدِينَ** "Some of them believe in it, and some of them do not believe in it, and your Lord knows best the agents of corruption." (10:40). He divided them into two groups: those who believe in the Quran and those who do not. Then He alluded to those who do not believe as being "agents of corruption" (مفسدون). From this, it is concluded that those who deny what is in the Quran only do so because they are agents of corruption.

Thus, the verse is to clarify the state they are in—the belief of some and the disbelief of others—and that disbelief arises from the vice of corruption (الإفساد).

As for what some have mentioned in the commentary of the verse—that the meaning is: "Your people will not be like those wrongdoers before them who denied their messengers except for a few, and whose end was the punishment of extirpation; rather, your people will be two groups: a group that will believe in this Quran and a group that will never believe"—this is a meaning entirely outside the indication of the verse.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَإِنْ كَذَّبُوكَ فَقُلْ لِي عَمَلِي وَلَكُمْ عَمَلُكُمْ** "If they deny you, say, ‘My deeds belong to me and your deeds belong to you’" (10:41) to the end of the verse. This is a formal instruction (تلقين) on disavowal (التبري) in the event of their denial of him. It is one of the stages of championing the truth by one who has risen to revive it. The way is to urge people toward it if they can be urged; otherwise, one must disavowal them so they do not hold him responsible for their falsehood.

And His saying: **أَنْتُمْ بَرِيئُونَ مِمَّا أَعْمَلُ وَأَنَا بَرِيءٌ مِمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ** "You are quit of what I do and I am quit of what you do" (10:41) is an explanation of His saying: **لِي عَمَلِي وَلَكُمْ عَمَلُكُمْ** "My deeds belong to me and your deeds belong to you."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ يَسْتَمِعُونَ إِلَيْكَ أَفَأَنْتَ تُسْمِعُ الصُّمَّ وَلَوْ كانُوا لا يَعْقِلُونَ** "Some of them listen to you. But can you make the deaf hear even if they do not understand?" (10:42). The interrogation is for negation (الإنكار). His saying: **وَلَوْ كانُوا لا يَعْقِلُونَ** "even if they do not understand" is a contextual clue that what is meant by the negation of hearing is the negation of what accompanies it—the understanding of what the spoken word indicates, which is called the "hearing of the heart."

The meaning is: Among them are those who listen to you while they are deaf, having no hearing in their hearts; and you are not capable of making them hear when they possess no hearing.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ يَنْظُرُ إِلَيْكَ** "And some of them look at you" (10:43) to the end of the verse. The discussion regarding this is analogous to the discussion regarding the preceding verse.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَظْلِمُ النَّاسَ شَيْئاً وَلكِنَّ النَّاسَ أَنْفُسَهُمْ يَظْلِمُونَ** "Indeed Allah does not wrong people in the least; rather, it is people who wrong themselves." (10:44). This is intended to indicate that the deprivation of hearing and sight—in the form of deafness and blindness—afflicting these people is a result of their wronging themselves, without Allah, the Exalted, having wronged them by stripping them of hearing and sight. For they were only given what they brought upon themselves.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَيَوْمَ يَحْشُرُهُمْ كَأَنْ لَمْ يَلْبَثُوا إِلَّا ساعَةً مِنَ النَّهارِ يَتَعارَفُونَ بَيْنَهُمْ** "On the day He will gather them, [it will be] as if they had not stayed [in the world] but for an hour of the day, getting acquainted with one another." (10:45). The apparent meaning of the verse is that "the day" (يَوْمَ) is an adverbial phrase (ظرف) related to His saying: **قَدْ خَسِرَ** "certainly they are losers" etc. His saying: **كَأَنْ لَمْ يَلْبَثُوا إِلَّا ساعَةً** "as if they had not stayed but for an hour" is a circumstantial qualifier (حال) for the plural pronoun in "He will gather them" (يَحْشُرُهُمْ). His saying: **يَتَعارَفُونَ بَيْنَهُمْ** "getting acquainted with one another" is a second circumstantial qualifier explaining the first.

The meaning is: Those who denied the encounter with Allah have certainly lost on the day He gathers them to Him, while they deem this worldly life insignificant, considering it like a stay of an hour of the day, and they recognize one another without any of them denying or forgetting the other.

Some have mentioned that His saying: **كَأَنْ لَمْ يَلْبَثُوا** is an adjective for "day" or an adjective for the omitted verbal noun (المصدر) indicated by His saying: **يَحْشُرُهُمْ**. Others have mentioned that His saying: **يَتَعارَفُونَ بَيْنَهُمْ** is an adjective for "hour" (ساعة). These are remote possibilities not supported by the wording.

In any case, the verse contains a return to the discourse on the "encounter" (اللقاء) mentioned at the beginning of the Surah, and a shift back to what was mentioned just now: that it is expected that the interpretation (تأويل) of the religion will come to them.

It is as if it says: Even if the interpretation of the Quran has not yet come to them, they should not be deceived by remaining fixed upon the outward appearances of this worldly life, nor should they consider the duration long or the term slow. For they shall be gathered to Allah and will witness that the worldly life was nothing but a brief enjoyment, and the stay therein was but a short stay, as if they had not stayed but for an hour of the day, recognizing one another.

On that day, their loss in denying the encounter with Allah will become manifest to them through direct vision. This will occur through the arrival of the interpretation of the religion, the uncovering of the reality of the matter, the manifestation of the light of monotheism (التوحيد) as it truly is, and the clarity that the kingdom on that day belongs to Allah, the One, the All-Paramount (القهار), exalted is His state.
